items.question_text: Complete: A visual approach is an ATC authorization for an aircraft on an IFR flight plan to proceed visually and clear of clouds to the airport of intended landing. It is not a standard instrument approach procedure and has no _____ segment.
items.explanation: Per 7-4-1, a visual approach has no missed approach segment. This means a go-around from a visual approach does not follow a published missed approach procedure; instead, the aircraft is handled as any go-around with appropriate IFR separation provided.

choices.text: missed approach

items.question_text: Under 7-4-1a1, at airports with an operating control tower, what are the two options for directing an aircraft executing a go-around from a visual approach?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-1a, the two options are: (1) enter the traffic pattern for landing (no altitude assignment required; pilot climbs to pattern altitude and is responsible for terrain/obstruction avoidance), or (2) proceed as otherwise instructed by ATC (pilot complies with instructions; ATC is responsible to provide instructions for climb to minimum IFR altitude). Both require ATC to provide approved or visual separation from other IFR aircraft.

choices.text: Enter the traffic pattern for landing, or proceed as otherwise instructed by ATC.
Execute the published missed approach procedure, or request an IFR clearance back to the holding fix.
Climb to the MVA and hold, or cancel IFR and proceed VFR.
Return to the final approach fix and try again, or divert to the alternate airport.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-1a2, when a go-around aircraft is directed to 'proceed as otherwise instructed by ATC,' what is ATC responsible for providing?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-1a2, ATC is responsible to provide instructions to the pilot to facilitate a climb to the minimum altitude for instrument operations. This differs from a1 (enter traffic pattern) where no altitude assignment is required and the pilot climbs to pattern altitude.

choices.text: Instructions to facilitate a climb to the minimum altitude for instrument operations.
A full published missed approach procedure with holding instructions.
Only terrain and obstruction avoidance guidance.
A complete new IFR clearance to an alternate airport.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-1, who is responsible for terrain and obstruction avoidance during a go-around after a visual approach?
items.explanation: Per the 7-4-1 note, the pilot is responsible for their own terrain and obstruction avoidance during a go-around after conducting a visual approach. However, the facility can assign headings towards the lowest terrain and obstructions to assist.

choices.text: ATC, because the go-around returns the aircraft to full IFR separation and control.
The pilot, but the facility can assign headings towards the lowest terrain and obstructions.
Neither; terrain avoidance is automatically handled by the published missed approach segment.
Joint responsibility shared equally between ATC and the pilot.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-4-2, a vector for a visual approach may be initiated if the reported ceiling at the airport of intended landing is at least _____ feet above the MVA/MIA and the visibility is 3 miles or greater.
items.explanation: Per 7-4-2, the minimum ceiling for a visual approach vector is 500 feet above the MVA/MIA. This is the specific numerical threshold that must be met to initiate the vector.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-2, at airports without weather reporting service, what must exist before a vector for a visual approach may be initiated?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-2, at airports without weather reporting, there must be reasonable assurance that descent and flight to the airport can be made visually and clear of clouds — for example, using area weather reports, PIREPs, pilot requests, etc.

choices.text: Reasonable assurance that descent and flight to the airport can be made visually and clear of clouds.
A PIREP from at least two other aircraft confirming conditions.
A METAR from the nearest airport within 30 NM.
A pilot report of the airport in sight before the vector is initiated.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-2, what must the controller include in the phraseology when weather information is not available at the airport?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-2 phraseology, when weather information is not available, the controller includes 'WEATHER NOT AVAILABLE' with the vector clearance. This informs the pilot that no official weather is reported at the destination.

choices.text: WEATHER NOT AVAILABLE.
USE CAUTION, NO WEATHER AVAILABLE.
WEATHER UNKNOWN, PROCEED AT YOUR OWN RISK.
ADVISE WHEN READY TO COPY AREA WEATHER.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-4-3a, controllers may initiate, or pilots may _____, a visual approach.
items.explanation: Per 7-4-3a, either controllers may initiate or pilots may request a visual approach. This differs from some procedures (like VFR-on-top) that require a pilot request.

choices.text: request

items.question_text: Under 7-4-3 note, what authorizes towers to clear aircraft for visual approaches?
items.explanation: Per the 7-4-3 note, towers may exercise visual approach clearance authority when authorized by an LOA with the facility providing IFR service, or by a facility directive at collocated facilities.

choices.text: An LOA with the facility that provides the IFR service, or a facility directive at collocated facilities.
A blanket authorization in the facility SOP that applies to all towers regardless of facility type.
A verbal authorization from the TRACON supervisor on duty.
The tower's inherent authority as the controlling agency for the airport surface area.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-3a3, at locations with an operating control tower, what happens when the pilot reports the airport in sight but not the preceding aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-3a3, at towered airports when the pilot reports the airport or runway in sight but not the preceding aircraft, radar separation must be maintained until visual separation is provided — and visual separation is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super.

choices.text: Radar separation must be maintained until visual separation is provided, and visual separation is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super.
The approach clearance is withheld until the pilot reports both the airport and the preceding aircraft in sight.
The controller may issue the visual approach clearance immediately because the airport being in sight is sufficient.
Pilot-applied visual separation is automatically assumed once the airport is in sight.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-4-4b1, when conducting visual approaches to multiple runways, do not permit the respective aircraft flight paths to intersect unless approved separation is maintained. Ensure the intercept angle to the extended runway centerline is not greater than _____ degrees.
items.explanation: Per 7-4-4b1, the intercept angle to the extended runway centerline must not exceed 30 degrees. This is to reduce the potential for overshoots and preclude side-by-side operations with aircraft in a bank toward one another.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-4b2, when must approved separation be ensured during approaches to multiple runways?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-4b2, approved separation must be ensured until the aircraft assigned the higher altitude has descended through the altitude assigned to the lower aircraft, when the visual approach flight paths intersect.

choices.text: Until the aircraft assigned the higher altitude has descended through the altitude assigned to the lower aircraft.
Until both aircraft report the runway environment in sight.
Until one pilot reports the other aircraft in sight and accepts visual separation.
Until both aircraft have crossed the runway threshold.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-5, which of the following is NOT a condition for clearing an aircraft for a Charted Visual Flight Procedure (CVFP)?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-5, conditions are: operating control tower, published name of CVFP and runway in clearance, ceiling at least 500 ft above MVA/MIA (or if following another aircraft on the approach, the ceiling must be at or above the MVA), and the pilot affirms charted visual landmark or preceding aircraft in sight. A non-towered airport is not permitted for CVFP operations.

choices.text: An operating control tower is required.
The reported ceiling must be at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA.
The pilot must have at least two prior landings at the airport in the last 90 days.
The published name of the CVFP and the landing runway must be in the approach clearance.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-6, what must the reported ceiling be at the airport of intended landing before issuing an RVFP approach clearance?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-6c, the reported ceiling at the airport must be at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA and the visibility must be 3 miles or greater. This is the same weather threshold as vectoring for a visual approach under 7-4-2.

choices.text: At least the published circling minimums for the approach being substituted.
At least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA with visibility 3 miles or greater.
At least 1,000 feet above the MVA with visibility 5 miles or greater.
At or above basic VFR minimums for the airspace classification.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-6, who may use RNAV Visual Flight Procedures?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-6, RVFPs are special procedures flown in VMC and clear of clouds, used by authorized operators only. Authorization is determined by facility RVFP LOAs.

choices.text: Any IFR-rated pilot who requests the procedure.
Authorized operators only, as determined by facility RVFP LOAs.
Any aircraft equipped with an approved RNAV system.
Only military aircraft with mission authorization.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-4-7, a contact approach may be cleared only if the pilot has _____ it.
items.explanation: Per 7-4-7a, the pilot must request a contact approach. The controller may not initiate it. This is a mandatory pilot-request procedure, similar to VFR-on-top.

choices.text: requested

items.question_text: Under 7-4-7, what reported ground visibility is required before a contact approach may be authorized?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-7b, the reported ground visibility at the destination airport must be at least 1 statute mile. This is lower than the 3-mile requirement for visual approach vectors. If ground visibility is not available, flight visibility must be at least 1 statute mile.

choices.text: At least 3 statute miles.
At least 1 statute mile.
At least 5 statute miles.
No minimum; flight visibility is sufficient.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-7, what is the pilot responsible for maintaining during a contact approach?
items.explanation: Per the 7-4-7 note, the pilot is responsible for maintaining required flight visibility, cloud clearance, and terrain/obstruction clearance. Unless otherwise restricted, the pilot may deviate from the prescribed IFR pattern to proceed to the destination airport.

choices.text: Only terrain and obstruction clearance; ATC maintains cloud separation.
Flight visibility, cloud clearance, and terrain/obstruction clearance.
Only flight visibility; the published approach ensures cloud and terrain clearance.
Adherence to the full published instrument approach procedure without deviation.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-1b, what is expected of an aircraft executing a go-around from a visual approach at an airport without an operating control tower?

choices.text: Enter the traffic pattern for another visual approach attempt.
Execute the published missed approach procedure for the instrument approach most recently assigned.
Complete a landing as soon as possible or contact ATC for further clearance; ATC must maintain approved separation from other IFR aircraft.
Cancel IFR and proceed VFR to an alternate airport.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-1b, at non-towered airports, a go-around aircraft must complete a landing as soon as possible or contact ATC for further clearance. ATC must maintain approved separation from other IFR aircraft throughout.

items.situation: An aircraft on a visual approach at a towered airport executes a go-around. The controller needs to get the aircraft back into the IFR system quickly. The airport has a standard traffic pattern altitude of 1,000 feet AGL but the MVA is 2,500 feet MSL.
items.para_context: Per 7-4-1a, the controller may direct the go-around to: (1) enter the traffic pattern — no altitude assignment required, pilot climbs to pattern altitude and is responsible for terrain/obstruction, OR (2) proceed as otherwise instructed — ATC is responsible to provide instructions to facilitate a climb to the minimum IFR altitude.
items.question_text: Which go-around option should the controller select to get the aircraft to the MVA most reliably?

choices.text: Use option a2 to issue specific instructions facilitating a climb to the minimum IFR altitude, because the MVA exceeds pattern altitude and the controller is responsible for ensuring the aircraft reaches it.
Use option a1 directing the aircraft to enter the traffic pattern, because all go-arounds at towered airports must use the pattern.
Issue the published missed approach for the instrument procedure, because go-arounds from visual approaches must follow instrument missed approach segments.
Cancel the IFR flight plan because visual approaches have no missed approach segment, so the aircraft must proceed VFR.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-1a2, under option a2 ATC is responsible to provide instructions to facilitate a climb to the minimum altitude for instrument operations. Since the MVA exceeds pattern altitude, option a2 is more appropriate. Under a1, the pilot would climb to pattern altitude only (lower than MVA), which may not provide terrain/obstruction clearance for IFR operations.

items.instruction: The controller issued a vector for visual approach at an airport without weather reporting. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: TURN RIGHT HEADING TWO SEVEN ZERO, VECTOR FOR VISUAL APPROACH TO LINCOLN MUNICIPAL, WEATHER NOT AVAILABLE.

choices.text: Right heading two seven zero, visual approach to Lincoln, we have the weather.
Right heading two seven zero, vector for visual approach to Lincoln Municipal, weather not available.
Vector for visual approach to Lincoln, we'll get the weather from ATIS.
Heading two seven zero, cleared visual approach runway one eight.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-2 phraseology, the format is '(Ident) FLY HEADING or TURN RIGHT/LEFT HEADING (degrees) VECTOR FOR VISUAL APPROACH TO (airport name),' with 'WEATHER NOT AVAILABLE' added when appropriate.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-4, what must all aircraft be informed of when approaches are being conducted to parallel, intersecting, or converging runways?

choices.text: Only the specific runway each arrival will land on.
That approaches are being conducted to those runways; this may be accomplished through the ATIS.
That visual separation must be accepted by all arriving pilots.
That approaches are being staggered for wake turbulence only.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-4a, all aircraft must be informed that approaches are being conducted to parallel, intersecting, or converging runways. This can be accomplished through the ATIS broadcast, so individual advisories are not always needed.

items.question_text: Under 7-4-4 note, what is the purpose of the 30-degree maximum intercept angle to the extended runway centerline?

choices.text: To ensure aircraft maintain a standard rate turn throughout the visual approach segment.
To reduce the potential for overshoots of the extended centerline and preclude side-by-side operations with aircraft in a bank toward one another.
To comply with TERPS design criteria for visual approach surface protection.
To allow pilots to maintain visual contact with the runway environment during the turn to final.

items.explanation: Per the 7-4-4 note, the 30-degree intercept angle reduces potential for overshoots of the extended runway centerline and precludes side-by-side operations with one or both aircraft in a bank toward each other.

items.situation: A controller is clearing an aircraft for the 'RIVER VISUAL RUNWAY 27' CVFP. The reported ceiling is 600 feet above the airport, the MVA is 1,500 feet MSL, and the airport elevation is 500 feet MSL. The aircraft is not following another aircraft on the approach.
items.para_context: Per 7-4-5d, for an aircraft not following another on the approach, the reported ceiling must be at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA.
items.question_text: Can the controller issue the CVFP clearance?

choices.text: No, because for an aircraft not following another, the reported ceiling (600 ft above airport = 1,100 ft MSL) is not at least 500 feet above the MVA (1,500 ft MSL).
Yes, because the ceiling is at least 500 feet above the airport elevation, which is all that is required.
Yes, because the ceiling only needs to be above the MVA by any amount; the 500-foot margin applies only to following aircraft.
No, because CVFPs may only be issued when the ceiling is at least 1,000 feet above the MVA.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-5d, the 500-foot threshold is measured above the MVA/MIA, not above the airport elevation. With a ceiling of 1,100 ft MSL (600 ft AGL + 500 ft airport) and an MVA of 1,500 ft MSL, the ceiling is only 400 ft below the MVA — not 500 ft above it. The clearance cannot be issued.

items.instruction: The controller issued an RVFP clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: N123AB, CLEARED RNAV VISUAL RUNWAY TWO SEVEN APPROACH.

choices.text: Cleared the RNAV approach to runway two seven, N123AB.
Cleared visual approach runway two seven via RNAV, N123AB.
Cleared RNAV Visual Runway two seven approach, N123AB.
Cleared RNAV Visual Flight Procedure runway two seven, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-6 phraseology, the correct format is '(Ident) CLEARED RNAV VISUAL RUNWAY (number) APPROACH.'

items.instruction: The controller issued a contact approach clearance with an altitude restriction. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CLEARED CONTACT APPROACH, AT OR BELOW THREE THOUSAND, PROCEED DIRECT TO THE AIRPORT. IF NOT POSSIBLE, MAINTAIN THREE THOUSAND AND ADVISE.

choices.text: Cleared the visual approach below three thousand, N123AB.
Cleared contact approach at or below three thousand direct, N123AB.
Contact approach approved, descending to three thousand, N123AB.
Cleared contact approach, canceling IFR below three thousand, N123AB.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-7 phraseology, the format is 'CLEARED CONTACT APPROACH, AT OR BELOW (altitude) (routing). IF NOT POSSIBLE, (alternative procedures), AND ADVISE.'
